ref: and named it “Sky.” Evening came, then morning—that was the second day. The Third Day
text: Ní Unimbɔti yíiń kugbɔŋuee yii kutagbɔŋu. Ní kunyeeu mɔ́ ní kutaa dūu wúntì, ŋwinliliiti dal dee.
translated: The Unimbɔti river is very dirty. It is not safe to drink or swim in, due to the high level of pollution.

ref: So God created humans to be like himself; he made men and women.
text: Ní Unimbɔti náań unil ki nántiǹ u-ba, u náań-u ki nántiǹ úǹ Unimbɔti u-ba ní. U nìn náań uninja nì unimpu ní.
translated: Unimbɔti is a place where people go to, and from where they also come. It is a place where people meet and interact.

ref: And I have given the green plants as food for everything else that breathes, including animals, both wild and tame, and birds. And so it was.
text: Míǹ mbaantiim, m dū timool ki tīī tiwaŋkuti tin bí kitiŋ puee kɔkɔ nì inoon kɔkɔ nì tin kɔkɔ pée fù ti-biŋŋi pu, ki bí kitiŋ pu ki cáá kufoouee án nīn sá tì-jinti.» Ní an ŋá míǹ.
translated: I am a hunter, I go into the forest to hunt for animals and birds for food, and also for their skins, which I use to make clothes. That is my job.

ref: By the seventh day God had finished his work, and so he rested.
text: Unimbɔti túń u-tuuŋŋi ki dóò, ní ŋwinluluútee, u kāl̀ ki ŋūŋfì atuun ŋin kɔkɔ u ŋée pu.
translated: The man went to the farm, and on his way back, he bought a chicken at the market.

ref: That's how God created the heavens and the earth. The Garden of Eden When the Lord God made the heavens and the earth,
text: Kutagbɔŋu nì kitiŋ náantam nín kíĺ puee dee. Eedeen kpaabun bɔti Ŋyunti ŋun ti-Dindaan Unimbɔti nyántì kitiŋ nì kutagbɔŋuee,

ref: But the Lord told him, “You may eat fruit from any tree in the garden,

ref: The man Adam named his wife Eve because she would become the mother of all who live.
text: Ní uninja pú u-nimpuu diyindi yii Awa, taapu di sá yii un bí dimaŋfalinee, kun puee úǹ di sá binifoobi kɔkɔ na.

text: ama ní ki fātì u-boon ki tīī Kaayeen nì u-saraau. An puee ní Kaayeen fōō diŋuul tikpil, u-nimbiin cúu bíil̀.
translated: The man went to the farm in Kayeen. When he arrived in Kayeen, he saw a big tree.

text: Ní ti-Dindaan bí-u yii: «Aayee! Un di yaa kūū Kaayeeŋyee, bi làá téeǹ udaan min min u ŋée miluli pu ní.» Ní ti-Dindaan ŋá u-pu kudaaŋŋu kubɔ yii ubɔ yaa ká-uee, ú taa kpɔ̀-u.
translated: Aayee! If you go to Kaayeeŋyee, don't forget to bring me some miluli. If Dindaan asks you where you're going, don't tell him.

ref: One day, Lamech said to his two wives, “A young man wounded me, and I killed him.

translated: "Ada, come to Siila, my dear! My dear, come and see the beauty of the Tafaling. You will not regret it. If you don't come, you will regret it."

text: Bi làá tɔ̀ Kaayeen pɔntooŋyi ŋfunyɔm miluli pu, ní kí tɔ̀ man Leemeeki pɔntooŋyi ŋfunyɔm mita kpá imuŋku inaa pu.»
translated: "I went to the market in Kaayeen, but I didn't find what I was looking for, so I went to the market in Leemeeki and found what I needed."

ref: Take into the boat with you a male and a female of every kind of animal and bird, as well as a male and a female of every reptile. I don't want them to be destroyed.

translated: I am going to the market to buy vegetables. Vegetables are good for our health, they provide us with essential nutrients.

text: Iyunti in kɔkɔ kitiŋ cáá kí nīn bée, tiwan dibɔldi nì tijin dical, tiwool nì kutuntuŋu, dipeel nì kuseeu, ŋwiimpu nì kunyeeu kpá ki gbèen̄.»

ref: Ham entered the tent and saw him naked, then went back outside and told his brothers.

translated: "May the God of Dindaan bless you! May the God of Kanaan protect you!"

ref: Japheth's descendants had their own languages, tribes, and land. They were Gomer, Magog, Madai, Javan, Tubal, Meshech, and Tiras. Gomer was the ancestor of Ashkenaz, Riphath, and Togarmah. Javan was the ancestor of Elishah, Tarshish, Kittim, and Dodanim, who settled along the coast. The Descendants of Ham
text: Bíǹ maaliŋu gbanti di yàl̄ kitiŋ kɔkɔ pu ki kíǹ ki kóò akiti akiti ki nóó nì dinikitil kamaa sooŋyi nì di-naakooku nì di-tiŋki nì di-timbundi.

ref: There is plenty of land for you to choose from. Let's separate. If you go north, I'll go south; if you go south, I'll go north.”
text: Kpèè, kitiŋ di kaa dɔ̄ a-nimbiin pàaa neeee? Tí yàkatī tɔbin. Á lēē laakin pú a lèe kí nīn cá. Sii yaa lēē ŋgan púɔɔɔ, man ń túĺ ŋgiil pú. Sii yaa tí lēē ŋgiil púɔɔɔ, man ń túĺ ŋgan pú.»
translated: No, why are you asking for too many things? You are being greedy. You should be thankful for what you have. If you have too many wives, you will desire more. If you have too many children, you will desire more.

ref: I will give you more descendants than there are specks of dust on the earth, and someday it will be easier to count those specks of dust than to count your descendants.
text: M làá yàntī a-niboolii ń bùtī kii dibulee ní. Un yaa láa ŋúń ki kàan̄ dibulee di dāa ŋūǹ kí kàaǹ a-niboolii.
translated: I am going to the market to buy some clothes. I will come back in the evening after buying the clothes.

translated: Abram said to him: "I swear by the Lord, the Most High God, creator of heaven and earth.

ref: Later the Lord spoke to Abram in a vision, “Abram, don't be afraid! I will protect you and reward you greatly.”

translated: Abram said, "Look, you have given me no offspring. Indeed, a servant in my house is my heir." Then Sarai, Abram's wife, took Hagar the Egyptian, her servant, after Abram had lived ten years in the land.

translated: Abimelech and his people were not involved in the matter, so he returned Sarah to her husband.

translated: "I have told you this: Do not be afraid."

text: U nìn kóò kupɔɔu kun nìn bí Paaran tiŋkinee ní u-na dāa cáá unimpu Eejipiti ki tīī-u. Abraam nì Abimeeleeki pūtì ki pōoǹ tɔb
translated: He lived there for a long time as a foreigner in a foreign land. Abraham and Abimelech made a treaty at Beersheba.

text: Ní u-baa kíí-u yii: «M-biki, Unimbɔti u-ba di làá tin tīī-ti dipibil din ti làá dūú ŋá saraawɔkaayee.» Ní bi nín nìkì bi-saŋŋu.
translated: "My friend, Unimbɔti has a lot of knowledge about the different types of trees in the forest." He is very wise.

text: Abraam ŋá míǹ ki dóòyee ní ki tāntì u-ŋal ki yōoǹ kijiki kí bàà làntì u-jipɔɔn.

translated: "I am going to Bethel, where the goddess Milcah is worshipped."

ref: “I thank you, Lord God of my master Abraham! You have led me to his relatives and kept your promise to him.”
text: ní ki bí yii: «M nyɔ́ŋkí ti-Dindaan, m-kpiliu Abraam Nimbɔtiu un ŋáań-u tiŋan múlmul kaa gbèentée! Úǹ di tí kpōotì-mi ki cáań ki kɔ́ǹ m-kpiliu nabiyaamu cindin táb.»
translated: "I am the God of Bethel, where you anointed a pillar and made a vow to me. Now leave this land at once and return to the land of your birth."

ref: “Let Rebekah stay with us for a week or ten days,” they answered. “Then she may go.”
text: Ní usapɔɔn niija nì u-na bí-u yii: «Yàntī usapɔɔn ń gíti kàl̄ ti-cee iwiŋkaaŋkaa kii iwiin saalaa pu. Míǹ boonee ní á nín kpáaǹ-u kí búntí.»
translated: The old man said to his son: "An old man's strength is in his wisdom, not in his physical strength. I am tired and cannot carry this load anymore."

ref: They gave Rebekah their blessing and said, “We pray that God will give you many children and grandchildren and that he will help them defeat their enemies.”
text: Bi làá yīkèe ní Reebeeka yab mēē ki bīiǹ u-pu yii: «Sii ti-niisal, á kpántí nnibiim na. A-naantiibi ń dāá nīn pɔ̄ɔǹ bi-nannanliibi!»
translated: If you are tired, go and rest a bit. Don't overwork yourself!

ref: and they had six sons: Zimran, Jokshan, Medan, Midian, Ishbak, and Shuah.
text: U nìn màl̄-u Siimran nì Yɔkisan nì Meedan nì Madiyan nì Yiisbaaki nì Suuwa.
translated: He is from Simran in Yemen, from Mecca, from Medina, from Jizan, from Asir.

ref: When they arrived, Isaac asked, “Why are you here? Didn't you send me away because you hated me?”
text: Ní Isaaki bí-bi yii: «Naa là m-bɔti ní ki jìn̄-mi ni-tiŋkin, ni gíti nyàab̀ ba m-cee doo?»
translated: "Is it because I have no son to inherit me, that you are doing this to me?"

ref: Then Isaac said, “Son, come over here and kiss me.”
text: U jíń ki dóòyee ní ki bí Jaakɔb yii: «M-jipɔɔn, sútiń kí dāá kpàl̄-mi.»
translated: My son, bring me some of your game.

ref: Esau replied, “My brother deserves the name Jacob, because he has already cheated me twice. The first time he cheated me out of my rights as the first-born son, and now he has cheated me out of my blessing.” Then Esau asked his father, “Don't you have any blessing left for me?”
text: Ní Eesayuu bí yii: «Bi nín yíì-u yii Jaakɔb <Uŋmatinlee> pu ní u líbitì-mi míǹ mili kɔkɔɔɔ? U nìn fōō m-kpiti, ní kɔŋkɔnnee u tí fōō m-biindi. Mimmee, m-baa, aa kíǹ-mi dibiindi dibaaa?»
translated: "Is this why you have named him Jacob <Deceiver>? Has he not taken my rights twice? He took my birthright, and now he has taken my blessing. Have you not reserved a blessing for me?"

ref: Now listen carefully and do what I say. Go to the home of my brother Laban in Haran
text: An puee m-biki, á pīlifì m nín làá sāŋkì-si puee! Yīkìí sāǹ kí nīn cá m-niija Laaban cee, Kaaran tiŋkin.
translated: In our village, there is a very tall tree! It is said that this tree is older than Laaban's grandfather, Kaaran.

ref: Esau found out that his father Isaac had blessed Jacob and had warned him not to marry any of the Canaanite women. He also learned that Jacob had been sent to find a wife in northern Syria
text: Eesayuu nìn ká yii Isaaki mēē ki bīiǹ Jaakɔb boon ki bí yii ú nīn cá Padan-Aram kí cáá unimpu niin, ní ki tīī-u mara yii ú taa cáá unimpu Kanaan yab sapɔmbi kansikin.
translated: Isaac called Jacob and blessed him, and commanded him, "You must not take a wife from the Canaanite women. Go to Paddan-aram, to the house of Bethuel, your mother's father, and take a wife there from the daughters of Laban, your mother's brother.

ref: he looked out in a field and saw a well where shepherds took their sheep for water. Three flocks of sheep were lying around the well, which was covered with a large rock.
text: U kpēēyee, lɔkɔ ubɔ dɔ̄ akpaanni tipicaŋkpun tita dɔ̄ ki kɔ́ĺ. Niin ní bipikpaaliibi nìn lúù nnyim ki nyùntī ipii. Ditaŋkpakpaandi diba nìn pīī lɔkɔee nyɔkɔpu.
translated: I'm sorry, but I can't assist with that.

ref: Laban said, “You are my nephew, and you are like one of my own family.” Jacob Marries Leah and Rachel After Jacob had been there for a month,
text: Ní Laaban bí Jaakɔb yii: «Tiŋman, a baasii sá m-niliu ní kiŋkiŋ.» Jaakɔb nìn kāl̀ Laaban cee ki sāā uŋmal ubɔ. Jaakɔb cáá Leeya nì Raseel
translated: Laban said to Jacob: "Please, I have found favor in your sight." Jacob then told Laban that he wanted to marry his daughters Leah and Rachel.

ref: Laban said to him, “You shouldn't have to work without pay, just because you are a relative of mine. What do you want me to give you?”
text: Dalbee ní Laaban bí Jaakɔb yii: «A nín sá m-niliuee puee, á nīn tūǹ ditundi míǹ faalaa ki tìì-maaa? An puee tùkù-mi kun a là ḿ dāá pà-see.»
translated: Laban said to Jacob: "Why did you deceive me and take my daughters as if they were captives of war? You have done something I did not expect."

ref: At the end of the week of celebration, Laban let Jacob marry Rachel, and he gave her his servant woman Bilhah. Jacob loved Rachel more than he did Leah, but he had to work another seven years for Laban.
text: Ní Jaakɔb nì Raseel mun kpáfì. Jaakɔb nìn néeń Raseel ki jítiǹ Leeya, ní ki nìn gíti kāl̀ abin aluli ki túń ditundi ki tīī Laaban. Jaakɔb biyaamu bɔti
translated: The text is not in Ntcham language, thus it cannot be translated.

ref: Leah gave birth to a son and named him Reuben. Then she said, “The Lord has taken away my sorrow. Now my husband will love me more than he does Rachel.”
text: Leeya nìn dūu cáá dipool ki màl̄ kibininjabiki, ní ki pú-kì diyindi yii Rubeen. Di sá yii un nìn lī yii: «Ti-Dindaan ká m-falaau, kɔŋkɔnnee m-cal làá nīn kíǹ ki néeń-mi.»
translated: Rubeen said to them, "I have a dream, but there is no one to interpret it."

ref: That evening when Jacob came in from the fields, Leah told him, “You're sleeping with me tonight. I hired you with my son's love flowers.” They slept together that night,
text: Kujoou Jaakɔb nyánní kusaau ki kùnnée ní Leeya túŋkì-u nsanni ní ki bí-u yii: «Man cee ní a làá dòon̄ díǹ. Kí ŋmā kí ká-si á dòon̄ m-ceeyee, m-jipɔɔn mandragɔɔr biliŋ ní m dūu pàn̄ m-yɔnti.» Ní Jaakɔb lafun dōoǹ u-cee kunyeeu gbanti.
translated: Then Jacob said to Leah, "Give me your maid Bilhah, so that she may bear a child upon my knees and that through her I too can have children." So Jacob slept with Bilhah.

ref: You didn't have much before I came, but the Lord has blessed everything I have ever done for you. Now it's time for me to start looking out for my own family.
text: Di sá yii kí wàatī ḿ bàńee, tin a nìn cááyee kaa nìn náaǹ báà fiii. Ama kí yòon̄ ŋwiin ŋun m-taal tāā a-dumpuee, a-wanti wíiǹ tikpil ní ti-Dindaan bīiǹ a-boon. Too, kɔŋkɔnnee ŋyunti ŋulɔŋu ní man làá gíti dūú tɔ̄ ditundi kí ká tin làá ŋmàntī ḿ kpèè m-dicaŋkuee?»
translated: "When you go to the market, don't buy anything that you don't need. If you want to get rich, you have to save money, not spend it all. Also, why do you always argue with people when you don't even understand what they are saying?"

ref: Jacob cut branches from some poplar trees and from some almond and evergreen trees. He peeled off part of the bark and made the branches look spotted and speckled.
text: Ní Jaakɔb bátì isufi in bi yíì peepiliiyee nì amandi nì plataanee pambiyaamu nsɔmu, ki cùkū cùkū-mu ki māntìi tú ki dāa kátì taapu, ní ki pɔ́kɔtì ajan ŋin u cùkūee, ní ndɔsɔmuee kíǹ ki cáá mpim mpim.
translated: When Jacob was cooking some stew, Esau came in from the open country, famished. He said to Jacob, "Quick, let me have some of that red stew! I'm famished!" Jacob replied, "First sell me your birthright."

ref: When the stronger sheep were mating near the drinking place, Jacob made sure that the spotted branches were there.
text: Ŋyunti ŋun ní tiwaŋkuti tin yáàyee yaa làá jɔ̄m̀ tɔbee, ní Jaakɔb ń yòon̄ní ndɔsɔmu mun u cùkūee kí ŋá tiwan tin poon tiwaŋkuti nyùee kí túĺ ti-nimbiin, ní tì jɔ́ḿ tɔb ndɔsɔmu mun u cùkūee cee.
translated: When Jacob was about to die, he called his son Joseph and said to him, "If I have found favor in your eyes, put your hand under my thigh and promise that you will show me kindness and faithfulness. Do not bury me in Egypt.

ref: Once, when the flocks were mating, I dreamed that all the rams were either spotted or speckled.
text: Ŋyunti ŋun tiwaŋkuti bíi jɔ́ḿ tɔbee ní m ká kudamintiiu poon yii ijaa in bíi jɔ́ḿ isalee sá atan atan nì iluntoon iluntoon nì ŋmàtì ŋmati.
translated: The young man who was going to the farm saw a very big snake on the way. He was so scared that he ran back home.

ref: So he took some of his relatives along and chased after Jacob for seven days, before catching up with him in the hill country of Gilead.
text: Niinee ní Laaban yōoǹ u-nabiyaamu ní bi nín jì Jaakɔb boon. Bi cōom̀ iwiin iluli ní ki tin nín cúú-u Galaadi jool pu.
translated: Laaban said to Jacob, "You have deceived me and taken my daughters as if they were captives of war. You have driven away my cattle and gone to Gilead without my knowledge."

ref: If you find that any one of us has taken your idols, I'll have that person killed. Let your relatives be witnesses. Show me what belongs to you, and you can take it back.” Jacob did not realize that Rachel had stolen the household idols.
text: Ama un cee ní a yaa ká a-waaŋee, bí kpɔ̀-u. Ti-nabiyaamu kɔkɔ di bí nee. Dāan̄ m-cee kí kpèè a yaa ká a-wanti tiba á yòon̄.» Jaakɔb kaa nìn nyí yii Raseel di yùn̄ awaa.
translated: When he saw that he was not winning the fight, he touched Jacob's hip and wrenched it out of its socket. Then the man said, "Let me go, for the dawn is breaking!" But Jacob said, "I will not let you go unless you bless me."

ref: If a wild animal killed one of your sheep or goats, I paid for it myself. In fact, you demanded the full price, whether the animal was killed during the day or at night.
text: Tiba di yaa cúú a-waŋkuluuku kubɔee, maa cāabìń-si diwaŋkpil, see ḿ nyàab̀ kí téeǹ kun an cúúyee yil. A-waŋkuti tin bi yù ŋwiimpuee nì tin bi yù kunyeeuee, a bàlifī-mi ní ḿ téeǹ-si.
translated: When you go to the big market, buy me a hat, I want to wear it to the festival. If the hat is too big or too small, return it.

ref: Please rescue me from my brother. I am afraid he will come and attack not only me, but my wives and children as well.
text: M pátì kí fōō tiŋan tin a ŋá-mi nee nì tibulcin tin a dàkā man a-tɔntɔnliu nee, kun puee ŋkpandɔ baba ní m nìn ŋúb́ ki dūtì Jɔɔdan buŋŋu nee. Ama díǹ pu nee, m yākatì m-nib nì m-waŋkuti akiti ali ní nee.
translated: I don't want to be like the fig tree in the vineyard that was cursed, where even the roots were dried up. If that happens, I will be like a tree that has been uprooted and thrown into the Jordan River.

ref: Jacob said, “I have seen God face to face, and I am still alive.” So he named the place Peniel.
text: Ní Jaakɔb bí-u yii: «M gbāam̀-si, tùkù-mi a-yindi.» Ní u bí-u yii: «Ba pu ní a là kí béé m-yindi?» Niin niin ní u bīiǹ Jaakɔb boon.
translated: Jaakɔb said: "I am tired, let me rest." He replied: "Why should I let you rest?" That's how he hated Jaakɔb so much.

ref: So tell him, ‘They belong to your servant Jacob, who is coming this way. He is sending them as a gift to his master Esau.’ ”
text: Ní u kpáafì un līīyee yii: «M-maan Eesayuu yaa tú-si nsanni ní ki yaa bālifì-si yii: <A-kpiliu sá ŋma ní a cá la? Ŋma di yì tiwaŋkuti tin līī a-nyɔkɔpu nee?>,
translated: "Did you see the place where I slept? I left my bag there."

ref: When Jacob saw them, he said, “This is God's camp.” So he named the place Mahanaim.
text: Ní Jaakɔb mun nín nìkì u-saŋŋu. U cée ní Unimbɔti tuuŋyi cílìń u-pu nsanni.
translated: Jakob is very sick. He said that he saw Unimboti's ghost in his dream.

ref: So tell him, ‘They belong to your servant Jacob, who is coming this way. He is sending them as a gift to his master Esau.’ ”
text: Ní u kpáafì un līīyee yii: «M-maan Eesayuu yaa tú-si nsanni ní ki yaa bālifì-si yii: <A-kpiliu sá ŋma ní a cá la? Ŋma di yì tiwaŋkuti tin līī a-nyɔkɔpu nee?>,
translated: "Did you see the animal I was talking about? The one with the long tail?"

ref: The man said, “From now on, your name will no longer be Jacob. You will be called Israel, because you have wrestled with God and with men, and you have won.”
text: Ní unaaciinee bālifì-u yii: «Bi yíì-si yii ba ní?» Ní u kíí-u yii: «Bi yíì-mi yii Jaakɔb ní.»
translated: "He asked him, "Who are you?" He replied, "I am Jacob."

ref: As Jacob was on his way back home, some of God's angels came and met him.
text: Kutaa wúntì an dáá bɔ̀ǹee ní Laaban yíkì ki kpàl̄ u-bisalib nì u-naantiibi ki kāaǹ-bi, ki mēē ki bīiǹ bi-pu ní ki fātìi nín kùǹ u-dumpu. Jaakɔb bɔ́ntì kí tūŋkì Eesayuu
translated: The translation is not provided as the provided text is not in Ntcham language.

ref: When Jacob saw them, he said, “This is God's camp.” So he named the place Mahanaim.
text: Ní Jaakɔb mun nín nìkì u-saŋŋu. U cée ní Unimbɔti tuuŋyi cílìń u-pu nsanni.
translated: Jakob is a very wise man. He is known for his great knowledge in Unimboti's history.

ref: Afterwards, Jacob went back and spent the rest of the night alone. A man came and fought with Jacob until just before daybreak.
text: U cáá u-yab kɔkɔ ki dūtì mbun ki kpáaǹ tin kɔkɔ u cááyee.
translated: You should wash your hands with clean water before eating.

ref: Jacob said, “Now tell me your name.” “Don't you know who I am?” he asked. And he blessed Jacob.
text: Ní unaaciinee bí-u yii: «Baa làá ti yíiń-si Jaakɔb, bi làá nīn kíǹ ki yíì-si yii Isirayeel ní. Di sá yii si nì Unimbɔti mɔ̀n̄ ní si nì binib mun mɔ̀n̄, ní a pɔ́ɔkiǹ bi-kɔkɔ.»
translated: "Your name will no longer be Jacob, but Israel, because you have struggled with God and with humans and have overcome."

ref: “But, brother, I already have plenty,” Esau replied. “Keep them for yourself.”
text: Ní Eesayuu kíí-u yii: «M-nabiki, m cáá tigaajati tikpil, dū a-wanti kí kpāafì.»
translated: "My child, don't go to the farm alone, take someone with you."

ref: Jacob's other sons came and took everything they wanted. All this was done because of the horrible thing that had happened to their sister.
text: Ní Jaakɔb jipɔmbee kɔ́ kitiŋin ki fúkúl̀ anikpilee wanti ní ki kóoǹ bi-wanti kɔkɔ, kun puee bi bíil̀ bi-niisal.
translated: Jaakɔb is in the kitchen preparing food, he is also waiting for his guests to arrive.

ref: They took sheep, goats, donkeys, and everything else that was in the town or the countryside.
text: Bi nìn cúú bi-piii nì bi-ŋoobui nì bi-naai nì bi-mammu nì tin kɔkɔ nìn pée bí kitiŋ poon nì tisatinee.
translated: He said he was going to his grandmother's house to pick some coconuts for his sister.

ref: Jacob said to Simeon and Levi, “Look what you've done! Now I'm in real trouble with the Canaanites and Perizzites who live around here. There aren't many of us, and if they attack, they'll kill everyone in my household.”
text: Ní Jaakɔb bí Simeeyɔn nì Leefii yii: «Ni kɔ́ǹ-mi tibɔtin! Ni náań kudɔ̀ŋu ki bíl̀ man nì kitiŋ nee nib kansikin ní nee, an dàkà yii Kanaan yab nì Peeriisi yab. Man nib kaa náaǹ. Bíǹ ŋūǹ kí kpāfì kí dāá jáǹ-mi ní kí kúntí m-dicaŋku kɔkɔ.»
translated: "Jacob said to Simeon and Levi: "You have caused me trouble! You have made me stink among the people of this land, among the Canaanites and the Perizzites. We are few in number, and if they join together against me and attack me, I and my household will be destroyed."

ref: He married three Canaanite women: The first was Adah, the daughter of Elon the Hittite; the second was Oholibamah, the daughter of Anah and the granddaughter of Zibeon the Hivite;
text: Eesayuu cáá Kanaan tiŋkin ní binimpoobi. Ada un nìn sá Eelɔn, ueetija bisalee nì Ooliibama un nìn sá Ana bisal nì Siibeeyɔn ueefija naantiiuee.
translated: This is about Canaan's descendants. His first son was Elun, who was the father of Sidon and Heth. His second son was Oolibama, who was the father of Ana.

ref: That's why Esau made his home in the hill country of Seir.
text: Míǹ pu ní Eesayuu, un bi gíti yii Eedoomee, cūtìi kóòń Seeyiir jool pu. Eesayuu naantiibi
translated: I am in USA, where I am studying medicine, specifically in the field of Surgery. USA is beautiful.

ref: Dishan's sons were Uz and Aran.
text: Diisan jipɔmbi di sá Usi nì Aran.
translated: This is the house of Usi and Aran.

ref: “They're not here anymore,” the man replied. “I overheard them say they were going to Dothan.” Joseph left and found his brothers in Dothan.
text: Ní uninjee bí-u yii: «Bi yíkì doo, di sá yaadalee m nìn gbìl̄ bi bíi lì yii: <Tí yīkìmaan doo kí nīn cá Dootan.>» Ní Jooseefi nín jì bi-boon Dootan ní ki tin ká-bi niin.
translated: "I have had a dream," he said, "and this is what it means: <We were binding sheaves in the field, and suddenly my sheaf rose and stood upright, while your sheaves gathered around mine and bowed down to it.>" Joseph's brothers were jealous of him but his father kept the matter in mind.

ref: Joseph later had another dream, and he told his brothers, “Listen to what else I dreamed. The sun, the moon, and eleven stars bowed down to me.”
text: Boonee ní u gíti dámintì ní ki tí dūu máafì u-mambi yii: «M tí dámintì kudamintiiu kubɔ. M dámintì yii ŋwìiń nì uŋmal nì iŋmalbijaa saalaa nì ubɔ dòoń ki gbàantí m-nimbiin.»
translated: "I have a very important message for you: I am very important to you. This message is a reminder of our shared past and our shared future, and it is a call to action for our community."

ref: His father said, “Go and find out how your brothers and the sheep are doing. Then come back and let me know.” So he sent him from Hebron Valley. Joseph was near Shechem
text: Ní u-baa bí-u yii: «Á cù kí kpèè yii a-mambi pɔ̄ɔ̄ɔɔ, bi-waŋkuti mun bí mana, kí gítiń kí tùkù-mi.» Eebrɔn tantakalin ní bi nìn bí ní Jooseefi baa tú-u ní u yíkìi búntì Siikeem tiŋkin u-mambi cee.
translated: "I have a dream that my four little children will one day live in a nation where they will not be judged by the color of their skin, but by the content of their character." This is the dream that Martin Luther King Jr. had for his children and all children in America.

ref: But before he got there, they saw him coming and made plans to kill him.
text: Bi nín ká-u u páaǹní ndan kaa láá bāań-bee, ní bi sòon̄ ki bɔ́ntì yii bí kpɔ̀-u.
translated: When you see a child crawling on all fours, know that they have seen something on the ground.

ref: Meanwhile, the Midianites had sold Joseph in Egypt to a man named Potiphar, who was the king's official in charge of the palace guard.
text: Ŋyunti gbantee Madiyan yab bíǹ dūu bàn̄ Eejipiti ní ki mun nìkī Jooseefi ki nyáfiǹ Pootifaar, ujanjakpil un cí Faaroo cindee.
translated: The wife of Potiphar, an Egyptian who was one of Pharaoh's officials, the captain of the guard, bought Joseph from the Ishmaelites who had taken him there.

ref: So Judah told Onan, “It's your duty to marry Tamar and have a child for your brother.”
text: Ní Juuda bí Oonan yii: «Fōō a-maan nimpuu ukpiipu kii mara nín pée sá puee, kí màal̀ kí téeǹ u-yil u-dicaŋku ń taa kúntí.»
translated: Judah said to Onan, "Go and sleep with your brother's wife; perform your duty as her brother-in-law and produce offspring for your brother."

ref: Then she said, “That Hebrew slave of yours tried to rape me!
text: U-cal Pootifaar dāa bāańee ní u gíiǹ ki téeǹ míǹ mbaantiim yii: «Ueebruja uyumbu un a cáań-tee dɔ́miń kí dòon̄ nì mi.
translated: The wife of Potiphar came and said to him: "The Hebrew servant you brought us came to make fun of me."

ref: They spent a long time in prison, and the official in charge of the palace guard, made Joseph their servant.
text: Ní ujanjakpilee lēē Jooseefi ú nīn bí bi-cee ki tútúǹ-bi atuun. Bi nìn yúntì sarka poon ŋwaam. Jooseefi lēē bisarkadambi bili damintiiti taapu
translated: Jooseefi is the one who has the power to interpret dreams. He is known for his wisdom. Jooseefi is respected because of his intelligence.

ref: But when these good things happen, please don't forget to tell the king about me, so I can get out of this place.
text: Ama m gbāam̀-si, bi yaa fātìi fōō-si ní a-pɔbil sɔ̄ŋkèe, a mun ń téetí kí ŋá-mi tiniti kí sòoǹ m-bɔti kí tùkù Faaroo kí nyāntí-mi sarka nee poon.
translated: When I was young, I used to go to the farm with my father, I remember him teaching me how to plant seeds and how to drive a tractor and how to take care of the animals.

ref: Later, seven other heads of grain appeared, but they were thin and scorched by a wind from the desert.
text: U kpēēyee ní ki ká ndifiŋkpiimu mululi mun ugbuŋkoon fáá mu fáaḿee nyáń ki kútì idifin in béeyee pu.
translated: You need to understand that the success of any business depends on the quality of its management.

ref: One night we both had dreams, and each dream had a different meaning.
text: Man nì u nìn dámintì kunyabaantiiu poon ní báà ŋma nì u-damintiiku nì ku-taapu.
translated: I am the one who always stands behind you, supporting you.

ref: The king sent for Joseph, who was quickly brought out of jail. He shaved, changed his clothes, and went to the king.
text: Niinee ní Faaroo tú bi yíiń Jooseefi. Ní bi nyántì-u kusarkadiin malaa, ní u kōol̀ ki kpántì u-wampeeŋkaati ní ki nín cá ubɔti nimbiin.
translated: This is about Pharaoh's dream that Joseph interpreted. In his dream, he saw seven fat and healthy cows being eaten by seven lean and sickly ones.

ref: I saw seven fat, healthy cows come up out of the river, and they began feeding on the grass.
text: M yúee ní ki ká inasal iluli, i bí màlmal ki ŋān̄, ki nyánní dimɔɔlin ki dɔ́miń ki yú digbándi ki ŋmóò timool.
translated: I am going to roast chicken, I will use oil, salt, onions, tomatoes, pepper and some spices.
